text: This is the case of a seventy-year-old female who presented to emergency room after a fall. The patient had multiple medical problems and included spinal stenosis for which she had an MRI five years prior and demonstrated an splenic cyst measuring eight centimeters as an incidental finding. A CT scan performed in the emergency room demonstrated that the splenic lesion had grown to a size of thirteen centimeters.
Representative CT scan images are shown. The CT scan results were consistent with a hemangioma of the spleen with internal bleeding.

Having that into consideration and the history of frequent falls, we're concerned for massive bleeding if the spleen would rupture after a fall. And therefore, an elective splenectomy was offered to the patient.
She received post-splenectomy vaccines. She was cleared by medicine, pulmonology, and cardiology. And the patient was then brought to the operating room for an elective surgery.

The surgeon begins the dissection by accessing the lesser sac, taking down the gastrocolic ligament. The dissection continues along the greater curvature of the stomach until reaching the upper pole of the spleen, exposing a very large splenic mass.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection and a vessel sealer device to detach attachments to the splenic lesion. The splenocolic ligament is dissected from medial to lateral, followed by the mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The robotic third arm provides medial retraction of the colon, allowing for enhanced articulation and dexterity of the robotic instruments during lung dissection in challenging areas.

The surgeon continues medial dissection along the gastrocodic ligaments using a vessel sealer. The large size of the splenic lesion visibly displaces surrounding tissues, facilitating the dissection process.

The surgeon uses robotic shears to dissect and isolate the splenic vein and artery, following the impression of the splenic artery. Multiple branches of the vessels are encountered during the procedure.
The surgeon injects endocyanin green intravenously and activates the firefly function of the robotic system, illuminating the vessels supplying the spleen at the hilum for clear identification.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the isolated and clipped splenic vessels, completing the dissection and isolation process.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the spleen by taking down the splenophrenic ligament. An extra large endocatch bag is used to contain the spleen, which is then extracted through a previous C-section incision.
This picture shows the size of the specimen removed. The patient did well postoperatively. She was discharged on post of day five and following the office.

Initial pathology report demonstrated an organizing clot, but an increased number of B cells were found. Therefore, the second opinion was requested. The final pathology report confirmed a low-grade lymphoproliferative disorder.
